questions.text: Whose childhood is described in this chapter?
questions.explanation: The chapter is an extract from A.P.J. Abdul Kalam's autobiography, 'Wings of Fire'.

questions.text: Where was Kalam born?
questions.explanation: He was born into a middle-class Tamil family in the island town of Rameswaram.

questions.text: What was Kalam's father's name?
questions.explanation: Jainulabdeen was Kalam's father, whom he describes as a man of great innate wisdom.

questions.text: What did Kalam's father possess, despite lacking formal education and wealth?
options: Great innate wisdom and true generosity of spirit
questions.explanation: Kalam credits his parents for his values of honesty and self-discipline.

questions.text: What did Kalam collect during the Second World War to earn money?
questions.explanation: There was a sudden demand for tamarind seeds in the market in 1939.

questions.text: Who was Samsuddin to Kalam?
questions.explanation: Samsuddin was his cousin who distributed newspapers in Rameswaram.

questions.text: How did Kalam help Samsuddin earn his first wages?
options: By catching newspaper bundles thrown from a moving train
questions.explanation: When the train halt at Rameswaram was suspended, bundles had to be thrown from the moving train.

questions.text: What did Kalam inherit from his father?
questions.explanation: He says he inherited honesty and self-discipline from his father.

questions.text: Who were Kalam's three close friends in school?
questions.explanation: All three were from orthodox Hindu Brahmin families.

questions.text: What did the new teacher at the elementary school tell Kalam to do?
options: To go and sit on the back bench
questions.explanation: The teacher could not stomach a Hindu priest’s son sitting with a Muslim boy.

questions.text: Who was Ramanadha Sastry's father?
options: Lakshmana Sastry, the high priest of the Rameswaram temple
questions.explanation: He was a very influential person in Rameswaram.

questions.text: What did Lakshmana Sastry tell the new teacher?
options: To apologize or leave the school
questions.explanation: He told the teacher not to spread the poison of social inequality and religious intolerance.

questions.text: Who was Sivasubramania Iyer?
questions.explanation: He was an orthodox Brahmin who tried to break social barriers.

questions.text: What happened the first time Kalam was invited to Sivasubramania Iyer's house for dinner?
options: Iyer's wife refused to serve Kalam in her kitchen
questions.explanation: She was horrified at the idea of a Muslim boy being invited to dine in her ritually pure kitchen.
She was horrified at the idea of a Muslim boy being invited to dine in her ritually pure kitchen.

questions.text: What did the science teacher do when his wife refused to serve Kalam?
options: He served Kalam with his own hands and sat down beside him
questions.explanation: He was not perturbed and set an example for his wife.

questions.text: What change did Kalam see when he visited the teacher's house the second time?
options: The wife took him inside her kitchen and served him food
questions.explanation: She had observed Kalam's behavior and changed her mind.

questions.text: Why did Kalam want to leave Rameswaram?
options: To study at the district headquarters in Ramanathapuram
questions.explanation: He wanted to pursue higher education.

questions.text: What did Kalam's father say about him leaving home?
options: He said a seagull flies across the sun alone and without a nest
He asked him to stay and farm
questions.explanation: He used the analogy of the seagull to explain that children must grow and find their own path.

questions.text: Kalam's family used to arrange boats with a special platform for carrying ________.
options: Idols of the Lord from the temple to the marriage site
questions.explanation: This shows the communal harmony in Rameswaram during his childhood.

questions.text: What did Kalam's mother and grandmother tell the children at bedtime?
options: Stories from the Ramayana and the life of the Prophet
questions.explanation: They told stories from both religions, showing a secular upbringing.

questions.text: Kalam was a ________ child.
options: Short boy with rather undistinguished looks
questions.explanation: He contrasts his looks with his 'tall and handsome' parents.

questions.text: Their ancestral house was built in the middle of the ________ century.
questions.explanation: The house was built in the middle of the 19th century.

questions.text: Where was the 'pucca' house made of limestone and brick located?
questions.explanation: The house was on the Mosque Street in Rameswaram.

questions.text: What did Kalam's father avoid?
questions.explanation: He lived a simple and austere life.

questions.text: The news of the Second World War was followed by Kalam through the headlines in:
questions.explanation: Dinamani was the local newspaper.

questions.text: India was forced to join the ________ during the war.
questions.explanation: India joined the Allied Forces as a British colony.

questions.text: What distinguished Kalam as a Muslim boy in school?
questions.explanation: He wore a cap which marked him as a Muslim.

questions.text: Ramanadha Sastry took over the priesthood from his father. What did Aravindan do?
options: Went into the business of arranging transport for pilgrims
questions.explanation: Aravindan moved into the transport business.

questions.text: What did Sivaprakasan become in his later life?
options: A catering contractor for the Southern Railways
questions.explanation: The three friends took up different secular and religious professions.

questions.text: The science teacher said, 'Once you decide to change the system, such ________ have to be confronted.'
questions.explanation: He was teaching Kalam how to deal with social resistance to change.

questions.text: What was the name of the place where the idols were carried?
questions.explanation: It was a pond called Rama Tirtha, near Kalam's house.

questions.text: Kalam's father told his mother that her children were the sons and daughters of:
questions.explanation: He quoted Khalil Gibran to comfort her about the children leaving home.

questions.text: How much money did Kalam earn for selling tamarind seeds for a day?
questions.explanation: A day’s collection would fetch him the 'princely sum of one anna'.

questions.text: What was the occupation of most people in Rameswaram?
questions.explanation: It was a holy island town dependent on pilgrims and the sea.

questions.text: Kalam describes his childhood as very ________.
questions.explanation: He felt he had a very stable and loving upbringing.

questions.text: What does 'unperturbed' mean in the context of the science teacher?
questions.explanation: He remained calm when his wife refused to serve Kalam.

questions.text: Which religious text did Kalam's father read?
questions.explanation: He was a devout Muslim who went to the mosque for evening prayers.

questions.text: Who said 'Your children are not your children'?
questions.explanation: Kalam's father quoted this famous line from the poet Khalil Gibran.

questions.text: What did the teacher's wife do when Kalam visited the second time?
options: She served him food inside the kitchen
questions.explanation: This shows the success of the teacher's effort to change her mindset.

questions.text: The story 'My Childhood' highlights the value of:
options: Communal harmony and breaking social barriers
questions.explanation: The various incidents show how different religions lived together peacefully and challenged prejudice.
